input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system under study** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **cyber-biophysical systems** (Document 7) and **automotive manufacturing systems**, including **vehicle platforms, battery systems, and industrial IoT-enabled manufacturing processes** (Documents 8, 9, 12, 14, 17). Key characteristics include:

1. **Cyber-Biophysical Systems (Document 7)**:
   - **Type**: Hybrid systems integrating **biological, physical, and cyber components** (e.g., human-machine interfaces, embedded sensors, and AI-driven control loops).
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Sensors, actuators, and real-time monitoring devices (e.g., IoT nodes, wearables, or industrial sensors).
     - **Biological Layer**: Biological entities (e.g., human operators, biological tissues, or microbial systems) interacting with the cyber-physical infrastructure.
     - **Cyber Layer**: Simulation models, AI/ML algorithms, and digital representations (e.g., physics-based simulations, neural networks, or rule-based systems).
   - **Operational Domain**: **Real-time monitoring, adaptive control, and predictive maintenance** in dynamic environments (e.g., healthcare, industrial automation, or autonomous systems).
   - **Key Suitability for Digital Twinning**:
     - **Dynamic Interactions**: Continuous bidirectional data flow between physical and digital domains (e.g., sensor-to-model synchronization).
     - **Multi-Disciplinary Modeling**: Requires **co-simulation** of biological, physical, and cyber processes (e.g., using **Modelica** or **FMI** standards for interoperability).
     - **Adaptive Learning**: Incorporates **AI-driven calibration** of digital models based on real-time biological/physical feedback (e.g., reinforcement learning or Bayesian updating).

2. **Automotive Systems (Documents 8, 9, 12, 14, 17)**:
   - **Type**: **Vehicle platforms** (e.g., electric vehicles, autonomous cars) and **manufacturing processes** (e.g., assembly lines, battery production).
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Vehicles with embedded sensors (e.g., LiDAR, IMUs, temperature/humidity sensors), battery cells (e.g., lithium-ion), and manufacturing equipment (e.g., CNC machines, robotic arms).
     - **Cyber Layer**: **Digital models** (e.g., CAD/CAM models, physics-based battery degradation simulations), **edge computing nodes** (Document 14), and **cloud-based analytics platforms**.
   - **Operational Domain**:
     - **Vehicle Domain**: **Autonomous driving**, **predictive maintenance**, and **real-time performance optimization** (e.g., energy efficiency, collision avoidance).
     - **Manufacturing Domain**: **Digital manufacturing twins** for **process optimization**, **defect prediction**, and **supply chain integration** (ISO 23247-1:2021).
   - **Key Suitability for Digital Twinning**:
     - **High-Fidelity Simulation**: Uses **multi-physics simulations** (e.g., **ANSYS Fluent** for aerodynamics, **COMSOL** for thermal management) and **AI-driven surrogates** (e.g., Gaussian processes, neural networks).
     - **Real-Time Synchronization**: Leverages **5G/edge computing** (Document 14) for **low-latency data transfer** between physical and digital twins.
     - **Standardized Frameworks**: Adheres to **ISO 23247** (Document 10, 14, 16) for **digital twin architecture**, including **data exchange protocols** (e.g., **OPC UA**, **ROS 2**) and **ontology-based semantics** (e.g., **OWL/SWRL** for knowledge representation).
     - **Battery Systems (Document 17)**: Focuses on **state-of-charge (SoC) and state-of-health (SoH) modeling**, requiring **high-resolution sensor data** (e.g., voltage/current profiling) and **degradation prediction models** (e.g., **equivalent circuit models** or **neural ODEs**).

3. **Industrial IoT (Document 12)**:
   - **Type**: **Discrete manufacturing systems** (e.g., assembly lines, logistics networks).
   - **Main Components**: **IoT-enabled machines**, **RFID/NFC tags**, and **cloud-edge architectures** for real-time monitoring.
   - **Key Suitability**: Enables **predictive maintenance**, **energy optimization**, and **quality control** via **digital shadowing** of physical assets.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT) services** provided in the documents are categorized by **functionality, target users, and technical capabilities**, primarily aligned with **ISO 23247** and **domain-specific requirements** (e.g., automotive, aerospace, industrial IoT). Key services include:

1. **Monitoring Services (Documents 7, 8, 9, 12, 14, 17)**:
   - **Functionality**:
     - **Real-time telemetry**: Continuous streaming of **sensor data** (e.g., vibration, temperature, voltage) from physical systems to the DT via **edge nodes** (Document 14) or **cloud gateways**.
     - **Anomaly detection**: **AI-driven outlier detection** (e.g., **Isolation Forest**, **LSTM autoencoders**) to identify deviations in **battery health** (Document 17) or **manufacturing defects**.
     - **Health monitoring**: **Predictive maintenance** for **automotive components** (e.g., **bearings, electric motors**) using **degradation models** (e.g., **Paris Law for fatigue analysis**).
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Maintenance engineers** (for **predictive maintenance alerts**).
     - **Operational managers** (for **real-time KPI dashboards**).
   - **Technical Capabilities**:
     - **Low-latency processing** (<100ms) via **edge computing** (Document 14).
     - **Federated learning** for **privacy-preserving anomaly detection** across multiple DTs (implied in cyber-biophysical systems, Document 7).

2. **Optimization Services (Documents 8, 9, 10, 14)**:
   - **Functionality**:
     - **Energy optimization**: **AI-driven control** of **electric vehicle (EV) charging schedules** or **industrial energy consumption** (e.g., **reinforcement learning for load balancing**).
     - **Process optimization**: **Digital manufacturing twins** optimize **assembly line layouts** or **supply chain logistics** using **multi-objective optimization** (e.g., **NSGA-II**).
     - **Trajectory planning**: For **autonomous vehicles**, the DT provides **real-time path optimization** via **graph-based algorithms** (e.g., **A*** or **Dijkstra’s**) integrated with **high-fidelity physics simulations**.
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Fleet managers** (for **EV route optimization**).
     - **Manufacturing planners** (for **production scheduling**).
   - **Technical Capabilities**:
     - **Co-simulation with physics engines** (e.g., **Gazebo**, **V-REP**) for **realistic trajectory validation**.
     - **Constraint satisfaction** using **MILP (Mixed-Integer Linear Programming)** for **resource allocation**.

3. **Prediction Services (Documents 7, 9, 15, 17)**:
   - **Functionality**:
     - **Lifetime prediction**: **Battery degradation forecasting** (Document 17) using **surrogate models** (e.g., **polynomial chaos expansion**, **neural networks**).
     - **Failure prediction**: **Cyber-biophysical system failures** (e.g., **human-machine interface errors**) via **causal inference models** (Document 7).
     - **Performance prediction**: **EV range prediction** under varying environmental conditions (e.g., **temperature, humidity**) using **physics-informed neural networks**.
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Researchers** (for **model validation**).
     - **End-users** (for **battery health warnings**).
   - **Technical Capabilities**:
     - **Uncertainty quantification** via **Bayesian neural networks** or **Monte Carlo simulations**.
     - **Transfer learning** to adapt models across **different vehicle/battery types** (Document 15).

4. **Control Services (Documents 7, 8, 14, 16)**:
   - **Functionality**:
     - **Closed-loop control**: **Real-time adjustment** of **EV powertrain parameters** (e.g., **torque distribution**) or **industrial robot trajectories** via **model predictive control (MPC)**.
     - **Adaptive control**: **Cyber-biophysical systems** adjust control policies dynamically based on **human operator behavior** or **environmental changes** (Document 7).
     - **On-orbit collision avoidance** (Document 16): **Space-based DTs** use **predictive control** to adjust satellite trajectories based on **debris detection models**.
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Autonomous vehicle developers** (for **dynamic control algorithms**).
     - **Aerospace mission planners** (for **orbital mechanics optimization**).
   - **Technical Capabilities**:
     - **Hybrid control schemes** combining **classical PID** with **deep RL**.
     - **Federated control** for **distributed DTs** (e.g., **swarm robotics**).

5. **Visualization Services (Documents 8, 9, 12, 15)**:
   - **Functionality**:
     - **3D/4D visualization**: **Interactive digital replicas** of **vehicles**, **manufacturing lines**, or **cyber-biophysical workflows** using **Unreal Engine** or **Blender**.
     - **Augmented Reality (AR)**: **Overlays of DT data** (e.g., **predicted failures**, **energy consumption**) onto physical systems via **AR glasses** (e.g., **Microsoft HoloLens**).
     - **Dashboarding**: **Real-time KPI visualization** (e.g., **Grafana**, **Power BI**) for **operational decision-making**.
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Technicians** (for **AR-guided maintenance**).
     - **Executives** (for **strategic analytics**).
   - **Technical Capabilities**:
     - **VR/AR integration** with **DT simulations** for **immersive training**.
     - **Dynamic data fusion** of **sensor data**, **AI predictions**, and **historical trends**.

6. **Knowledge Management Services (Documents 11, 13, 15)**:
   - **Functionality**:
     - **Ontology-driven semantics**: **Formal representation** of **DT components** (e.g., **OWL/SWRL**) for **semantic interoperability** (ISO/IEC 30173:2023, Document 11).
     - **Digital twin registry**: **Standardized metadata** (e.g., **ISO 23247**) for **discovery and reuse** of DTs across organizations (Document 13).
     - **Explainable AI (XAI)**: **Interpretability tools** (e.g., **SHAP values**, **LIME**) for **AI-driven predictions** in DTs.
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Data scientists** (for **model interpretability**).
     - **IT architects** (for **DT integration**).
   - **Technical Capabilities**:
     - **Linked Data principles** for **DT knowledge graphs**.
     - **Versioning systems** (e.g., **Git for DT models**).

7. **Security and Privacy Services (Implied in Documents 7, 14)**:
   - **Functionality**:
     - **Data encryption**: **End-to-end encryption** for **sensor-to-DT data transmission**.
     - **Federated learning**: **Privacy-preserving AI training** across multiple DTs (e.g., **secure multi-party computation**).
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Cybersecurity teams** (for **DT vulnerability assessments**).
   - **Technical Capabilities**:
     - **Blockchain for DT audit trails** (implied in cyber-biophysical systems).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tooling and enablers** for Digital Twin (DT) systems in the provided documents are **domain-specific**, leveraging **standards, frameworks, and technologies** to enable **real-time synchronization, AI integration, and interoperability**. Key components include:

1. **Standards and Frameworks (Documents 10, 11, 12, 14, 16)**:
   - **ISO 23247-1:2021** (Document 10, 14, 16):
     - **Digital Twin Architecture Framework**: Defines **layers** (e.g., **physical, cyber, business**) and **reference models** for **automotive and industrial DTs**.
     - **Data Exchange Protocols**: Specifies **OPC UA**, **ROS 2**, and **FMI (Functional Mock-up Interface)** for **interoperability**.
     - **Lifecycle Management**: **Phases** (e.g., **conceptualization, deployment, operation**) with **ontology-based semantics** (ISO/IEC 30173:2023, Document 11).
   - **ISO/IEC 30173:2023** (Document 11):
     - **Concepts and Terminology**: Standardizes **DT definitions**, **digital shadow**, and **digital thread**.
     - **Ontology Standards**: Uses **OWL/SWRL** for **knowledge representation** in DTs.
   - **Aerospace Adaptations (Document 16)**:
     - **ISO 23247 extension** for **on-orbit collision avoidance**, integrating **space debris detection models** with **DT simulations**.

2. **Simulation Engines and Co-Simulation Tools (Documents 7, 9, 12, 15)**:
   - **Multi-Physics Simulation**:
     - **ANSYS Fluent** (for **CFD in automotive aerodynamics**).
     - **COMSOL Multiphysics** (for **thermal/fluid dynamics in batteries**).
     - **Modelica** (for **energy system modeling** in cyber-biophysical systems).
   - **Co-Simulation Standards**:
     - **FMI (Functional Mock-up Interface)** (Document 12) for **seamless integration** of **physics-based and AI models**.
     - **Gazebo/ROS 2** (for **robotics and autonomous vehicle simulations**).
   - **AI/ML Surrogates**:
     - **Neural Networks** (e.g., **PyTorch**, **TensorFlow**) for **real-time predictions**.
     - **Gaussian Processes** (for **uncertainty-aware battery degradation models**).

3. **Edge and Cloud Computing Platforms (Documents 14, 15)**:
   - **Edge Computing (Document 14)**:
     - **NVIDIA Jetson** or **Intel Edge Insights** for **low-latency processing** of **sensor data** (e.g., **<100ms latency**).
     - **Federated learning** across **edge DTs** to preserve **privacy**.
   - **Cloud Platforms**:
     - **AWS IoT Greengrass** or **Microsoft Azure Digital Twins** for **scalable DT orchestration**.
     - **Kubernetes** for **containerized DT microservices**.

4. **Data Management and Databases (Documents 8, 9, 12, 17)**:
   - **Time-Series Databases**:
     - **InfluxDB** or **TimescaleDB** for **high-frequency sensor data** (e.g., **battery voltage profiles**).
   - **Graph Databases**:
     - **Neo4j** for **ontology-based DT knowledge graphs** (ISO/IEC 30173).
   - **Digital Twin Databases**:
     - **Siemens MindSphere** or **PTC ThingWorx** for **manufacturing DTs**.
     - **Custom DT repositories** (e.g., **PostgreSQL with JSONB**) for **metadata management**.

5. **AI/ML and Analytics Tools (Documents 7, 15, 17)**:
   - **Predictive Analytics**:
     - **Scikit-learn**, **XGBoost** for **classification/regression tasks**.
     - **Deep Learning Frameworks** (e.g., **PyTorch Lightning** for **neural ODEs** in battery modeling).
   - **Explainable AI (XAI)**:
     - **SHAP**, **LIME** for **interpreting AI-driven DT predictions**.
   - **Reinforcement Learning**:
     - **Stable Baselines3** for **control policies** in **autonomous vehicles**.

6. **Development and Integration Tools (Documents 12, 13, 15)**:
   - **Modeling Languages**:
     - **SysML** for **systems engineering** in DTs.
     - **UML** for **software architecture** of DT components.
   - **Version Control**:
     - **Git** for **DT model versioning** (Document 13).
   - **APIs and Middleware**:
     - **RESTful APIs** (e.g., **FastAPI**) for **DT service orchestration**.
     - **MQTT** for **lightweight IoT data streaming**.

7. **Specialized Technologies for Specific Domains**:
   - **Automotive (Documents 8, 9, 17)**:
     - **Battery Health Management Systems (BHMS)**: **Equivalent circuit models** + **neural networks** for **SoC/SoH prediction**.
     - **Autonomous Driving Stacks**: **NVIDIA DRIVE** or **Apollo Auto** for **perception + DT integration**.
   - **Cyber-Biophysical Systems (Document 7)**:
     - **Human-Machine Interface (HMI) Simulation**: **Unity/Unreal Engine** for **immersive DT training**.
     - **Biological Signal Processing**: **EEG/fNIRS sensors** integrated with **DT cognitive models**.
   - **Aerospace (Document 16)**:
     - **Space Debris Detection Models**: **Radar-based tracking** + **DT collision avoidance algorithms**.
     - **Orbital Mechanics Simulators**: **STK (Systems Tool Kit)** for **satellite trajectory optimization**.

8. **Security and Privacy Tools**:
   - **Encryption**: **TLS 1.3** for **secure DT data transmission**.
   - **Federated Learning Frameworks**: **TensorFlow Federated** for **privacy-preserving AI**.
   - **Blockchain**: **Hyperledger Fabric** for **DT audit trails** (implied in cyber-biophysical systems).

9. **Visualization and AR/VR Tools (Documents 8, 15)**:
   - **3D/4D Visualization**:
     - **Unreal Engine 5** or **Blender** for **interactive DT replicas**.
     - **ParaView** for **scientific visualization** of **multi-physics simulations**.
   - **AR/VR Integration**:
     - **Microsoft HoloLens 2** for **AR-guided maintenance**.
     - **Oculus Quest** for **VR-based DT training simulations**.

10. **DevOps and CI/CD for DTs (Document 15)**:
    - **CI/CD Pipelines**: **Jenkins** or **GitHub Actions** for **automated DT deployment**.
    - **Containerization**: **Docker + Kubernetes** for **scalable DT microservices**.
    - **A/B Testing**: **Canary deployments** for **DT model validation**.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination, adhering to stricter vehicular application standards despite hardware limitations. Temporal granularity is dictated by the **ROS2-based execution framework**, where each Function Entity (FE) is implemented as an individual ROS2 package containing ROS2 nodes. Latency requirements for services are implicitly governed by the **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA)**, which mandates real-time synchronization for safety-critical operations, though explicit service-level time scales (e.g., for data pre-processing, actuation, or Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking) are not detailed in the provided documents. The **Physical Twin Entity** handles time-sensitive operations like actuation and device communication, while the **Digital Twin Entity** manages control mechanisms with unspecified but likely lower-latency constraints compared to physical interactions.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system follows a **decentralized, entity-based hierarchical structure** with three primary entities: **Physical Twin Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, and **User Entity**, each deployed on separate hardware components. This design diverges from ISO 23247 RA by consolidating resource-specific and general automotive functionalities (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking) within the **Physical Twin Entity**, which contrasts with the standard’s **Operation Management Entity (OME)**. Multiplicities are not explicitly defined for multiple Digital Twin instances, but the architecture supports **scalable deployment** via ROS2 packages, where each FE is modular and can be instantiated independently. Coordination between entities relies on **ROS2 inter-node communication**, with no centralized orchestrator mentioned. Responsibilities are partitioned by functional domains: the **User Entity** manages visualization (3D Visualization) and control (Control Panel), while the **Digital Twin Entity** handles data management and control logic. The **Physical Twin Entity** bridges physical resources (sensors/actuators) and digital services.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system adopts a **ROS2-based microservices architecture**, where each Function Entity (FE) is implemented as an independent ROS2 package containing ROS2 nodes. This aligns with the **ISO 23247 RA’s entity-based structure**, but with deviations: the **Physical Twin Entity** extends beyond resource-specific operations to include general automotive functionalities (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking), deviating from the OME’s scope. Orchestration is achieved through **ROS2’s publish-subscribe model**, enabling asynchronous communication between entities. Resource management is distributed across hardware components, with no centralized orchestrator explicitly mentioned. The system integrates **sensors/actuators** (Physical Twin Entity) with **data pre-processing** and **control mechanisms** (Digital Twin Entity), while the **User Entity** provides a singular **User Interface FE** split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel components. The architecture emphasizes **modularity and entity ownership**, impacting engineering processes due to functional deviations from ISO 23247.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247** standard, which defines an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** where computational models and data are structured into **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**—physical entities (e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes) with observable attributes. The system employs **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)** to represent OMEs digitally, integrating **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** (explicitly not detailed in the document but implied by the RA’s functional decomposition). Data sources are managed via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which interfaces with OMEs for real-time data acquisition. The **Functional Entities (FEs)**—smaller modular components—handle specific tasks such as **data transformation, human-interpretable formatting, and user interaction**, though their implementation is unspecified (e.g., potential use of **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for interoperability). Model relationships are defined by the RA’s entity hierarchy, where each DTE depends on FEs for localized functionality, and data management follows a **decoupled, entity-specific approach** to ensure modularity and scalability. No explicit mention of data formats, storage methods, or model fidelity metrics (e.g., resolution, temporal granularity) is provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **ISO 23247** standard implicitly outlines fidelity and validity considerations through its **entity-based RA**, where **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)** must accurately reflect **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**. While the document does not specify **validation/verification methods** (e.g., statistical testing, simulation benchmarking) or **uncertainty quantification techniques**, it implies that fidelity is ensured via **functional decomposition** into **Functional Entities (FEs)**—each responsible for localized tasks like data transformation or control. Error detection/correction mechanisms are not detailed, but the RA’s modular design suggests **entity-level error isolation** and **user-driven quality assurance** via the **User Entity** interface. No explicit mention of **model calibration, uncertainty propagation, or formal quality assurance processes** (e.g., ISO 9001 alignment) is provided.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves real-time data acquisition from physical sensors (LiDAR, motor, steering servo) and vehicle state monitoring, followed by structured preprocessing and transmission to the digital twin. **Data types transmitted** include raw point cloud data (LiDAR), vehicle state metrics (e.g., motor actuation, steering servo angles), and clustered/tracked object data derived from point cloud processing. **Preprocessing steps** are executed by the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** SW component, which converts raw LiDAR point clouds (2D plane representations) into structured object tracks and driving path projections. **Data integration mechanisms** rely on the **Data Pre-Processing Functional Entity (FE)**, which standardizes and aligns data streams before serialization via the **Data Translation FE** (ISO 23247 compliant). **Communication protocols** for physical-to-virtual data transfer use **Wi-Fi (UDP transport layer)** for inter-entity communication, while intra-system SW components communicate via **ROS2**. **Transmission frequencies** are not explicitly stated but are implied to be high-frequency (e.g., LiDAR point clouds and vehicle state updates) to support real-time visualization and monitoring. **Data quality assurance** is inferred through the preprocessing pipeline, which ensures consistency in object tracking and path projection for downstream visualization (e.g., 3D Visualization FE). **Event triggers** for data capture include continuous sensor polling (e.g., LiDAR scans) and vehicle state changes (e.g., motor/servo actuation events).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this DTS is facilitated by the **Controlling FE (ACC)** and **Actuation FE**, enabling closed-loop control via command execution on physical actuators. **Command types** include **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) inputs**, which are serialized and translated through the **Data Translation FE** to align with physical actuator interfaces (e.g., motor, steering servo). **Control message formats** are standardized via the **Data Translation FE**, ensuring compatibility between digital twin logic and physical hardware. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not explicitly detailed, but the system implies reliance on **ROS2** for intra-component communication and **Wi-Fi/UDP** for inter-entity reliability, suggesting protocol-level integrity checks. **Feedback systems** are inferred through the **Data Collecting FE**, which captures post-actuation vehicle state data (e.g., motor/servo responses) for real-time adjustment in the digital twin. **Conflict resolution** is not explicitly described, but the **Controlling FE** likely prioritizes high-level ACC logic over lower-level actuation conflicts via centralized message routing. **Command execution confirmation** is implied through the **Actuation FE**, which processes translated commands and returns status updates to the digital twin for iterative control adjustments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **Digital Twin Technical Connection** architecture leverages a **multi-layered communication framework** combining **ROS2** for intra-system SW component interactions and **Wi-Fi (UDP transport layer)** for inter-entity communication between Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User Entities. **Network topology** follows a **distributed deployment**, where sensor data (LiDAR, motor/servo) propagates through **Data Pre-Processing FE** and **Actuation FE** before reaching the **Controlling FE** and **Visualization FE**. **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are critical for real-time operation, particularly for LiDAR point cloud processing and ACC control loops, though exact metrics (e.g., Mbps, ms latency) are not provided. **Reliability mechanisms** include **UDP-based redundancy** for inter-entity communication, ensuring continuous data flow despite transient network issues. **Security protocols** are not explicitly mentioned, but the use of **Wi-Fi/UDP** suggests reliance on standard IP-level security (e.g., encryption) rather than specialized DT-specific measures. The **Data Translation FE** acts as a critical bridge, serializing data streams and performing unit conversions to ensure cross-platform compatibility across ROS2 and Wi-Fi networks.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **Digital Twin Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is **edge-centric**, with components distributed across **Physical Twin (onboard vehicle)**, **Digital Twin (local/edge processing)**, and **User Entity (remote visualization/reporting)**. **Computational requirements** are met by the **Data Pre-Processing FE**, **Actuation FE**, and **Controlling FE**, which execute on-board or edge devices capable of handling real-time LiDAR clustering, object tracking, and ACC logic. **Storage requirements** are implied for **Data Storage FE**, which archives processed data (e.g., reports, vehicle state logs) and raw point clouds for post-processing. **Scalability mechanisms** are not explicitly detailed, but the modular **ROS2-based SW architecture** allows for horizontal scaling of components (e.g., additional Data Collecting/Reporting FEs) as needed. **Deployment models supported** include **on-premise (vehicle-mounted)** and **edge (local processing hubs)** but do not extend to cloud-based deployment. **Availability/reliability assurance** relies on **Wi-Fi/UDP redundancy** and **ROS2 fault tolerance**, ensuring continuous operation during network fluctuations or component failures. The system does not mention cloud integration or hybrid deployment strategies.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The lifecycle of the Digital Twin (DT) system, as defined in **ISO 23247 Part 2 (Reference Architecture)**, follows an **entity-based framework** structured around four primary roles: **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**, **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, and **User Entity**. Each lifecycle phase is governed by **transition management** between these entities, ensuring seamless data flow and functional alignment.

1. **Development Phase**:
   - **OME Definition**: The lifecycle begins with identifying and defining **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**, which include physical assets (equipment, facilities, products) and operational processes (personnel, workflows). These OMEs serve as the foundational observable units for the DT.
   - **Entity Decomposition**: The system decomposes OMEs into **Functional Entities (FEs)**, smaller modular components responsible for specific tasks (e.g., data actuation, human-interpretable transformation, or user interfacing). The standard does not prescribe implementation methods for FEs but suggests **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** as a potential protocol for interoperability.

2. **Representation Phases**:
   - **Physical-to-Digital Mapping**: The **Digital Twin Entity** creates a **digital representation** of the OME, maintaining a **bidirectional synchronization** with the physical counterpart. This representation evolves dynamically as the OME undergoes changes (e.g., upgrades, failures, or operational adjustments).
   - **Data Collection Phase**: The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** continuously gathers real-time or historical data from the OME via sensors, IoT devices, or embedded systems. Data formats are standardized to ensure compatibility with the Digital Twin Entity’s processing requirements.

3. **Operation and Maintenance Phase**:
   - **Runtime Synchronization**: The DT operates in a **closed-loop system**, where the Digital Twin Entity updates its model based on data from the OME, while the Data Collection Entity adjusts control actions (e.g., actuating machinery) to maintain alignment with operational goals.
   - **Version Control**: Although not explicitly detailed in ISO 23247, the lifecycle implies **versioned representations** of the DT to track changes (e.g., software updates, hardware modifications) and ensure traceability. The standard emphasizes **functional consistency** across versions to avoid disruptions.

4. **Lifecycle Governance**:
   - **Entity Interoperability**: Governance is achieved through **modular entity interactions**, where each entity (OME, Data Collection, Digital Twin, User) adheres to defined interfaces. The standard does not mandate governance frameworks but highlights the need for **clear ownership** of each entity’s responsibilities.
   - **Transition Management**: Transitions between phases (e.g., from development to operation) are managed via **well-defined handoffs** between entities. For example, the Data Collection Entity must validate data integrity before passing it to the Digital Twin Entity for processing.

The lifecycle is **domain-specific to manufacturing**, with no explicit mention of broader industrial or non-manufacturing applications in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **twinning process** and **Digital Twin (DT) evolution** in the context of **ISO 23247** are governed by an **entity-centric engineering framework**, where each **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)** is paired with a **Digital Twin Entity** for real-time or near-real-time synchronization. The process is structured as follows:

1. **Engineering Methodologies**:
   - **Entity-Based Development**: The DT is developed by decomposing the OME into **Functional Entities (FEs)**, each responsible for a specific function (e.g., data acquisition, transformation, user interaction). The standard does not prescribe implementation details but suggests **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** as a protocol for FE interoperability.
   - **Modular Design**: FEs are designed to be **independent and reusable**, allowing for incremental development and testing. This modularity facilitates **agile evolution** of the DT as new FEs can be integrated without disrupting existing functionality.

2. **Quality Assurance (QA) Practices**:
   - **Data Validation**: The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** enforces QA by validating data integrity, accuracy, and consistency before it is ingested by the Digital Twin Entity. While specific QA methodologies are not detailed, the standard implies **real-time anomaly detection** to ensure reliable data inputs.
   - **Functional Testing**: Each FE undergoes **unit testing** to verify its performance in isolation. The standard does not specify testing frameworks but emphasizes that FEs must adhere to **defined interfaces** to ensure seamless integration with other entities.

3. **Testing and Validation Approaches**:
   - **Bidirectional Validation**: The DT is validated through **closed-loop testing**, where the Digital Twin Entity’s outputs (e.g., predictive analytics, control commands) are compared against the OME’s physical responses. This ensures the DT’s **functional fidelity** to the real-world system.
   - **Scenario-Based Validation**: The standard suggests validating the DT under **operational scenarios** (e.g., equipment failure, process deviations) to assess its robustness. No specific validation tools or criteria are provided, but the focus remains on **real-world applicability**.

4. **Version Control**:
   - **Entity Versioning**: While not explicitly outlined, the lifecycle implies **versioned representations** of the DT to track changes (e.g., software updates, hardware modifications). Version control is critical for maintaining **traceability** and **auditability** of the DT’s evolution.
   - **Change Management**: Evolution is managed through **controlled updates** to FEs or the Digital Twin Entity, with transitions validated to prevent disruptions. The standard does not specify version control systems but highlights the need for **consistent governance** over DT updates.

5. **Evolution Management Strategies**:
   - **Incremental Updates**: The DT evolves through **iterative improvements**, where new FEs or enhanced functionalities are added incrementally. This approach minimizes downtime and ensures continuous operation.
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Evolution is governed by **entity-specific responsibilities**, where the Data Collection Entity ensures data consistency, the Digital Twin Entity maintains model accuracy, and the User Entity provides feedback for iterative refinement.
   - **Interoperability**: Evolution adheres to **standardized interfaces** (e.g., OPC UA) to ensure compatibility with existing systems and future upgrades. The standard does not mandate specific interoperability protocols beyond those implied by the entity framework.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The **insights and decision-making capabilities** of the Digital Twin (DT) system, as outlined in **ISO 23247**, are centered around **real-time analytics, user interaction, and confidence-driven decision support**, primarily facilitated by the **Digital Twin Entity** and **User Entity**. The system generates insights through **data-driven processing** and supports decision-making via **interactive interfaces**, though specific analytics methodologies or confidence measures are not detailed in the provided documents.

1. **Types of Insights Generated**:
   - **Operational Monitoring**: The DT provides **real-time insights** into the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**’s performance, including equipment health, process efficiency, and operational anomalies. These insights are derived from **continuous data collection** by the Data Collection Entity.
   - **Predictive Analytics**: While not explicitly mentioned, the DT’s architecture implies **predictive capabilities** (e.g., failure forecasting, maintenance scheduling) based on historical and real-time data. The Digital Twin Entity likely employs **statistical or machine learning models** (though unspecified) to generate these insights.
   - **Performance Optimization**: Insights include **process optimization recommendations**, such as adjusting parameters to improve yield, reduce downtime, or enhance resource utilization. These are derived from **comparative analysis** between the OME’s actual performance and its **digital representation**.

2. **Analytics and Processing Capabilities**:
   - **Data Transformation**: The Digital Twin Entity processes raw data from the OME into **human-interpretable formats**, leveraging **Functional Entities (FEs)** for tasks like normalization, aggregation, or visualization. The standard does not specify processing algorithms but implies **real-time or near-real-time analytics**.
   - **Decision Support Models**: The DT likely employs **rule-based or model-based decision support**, where insights are generated based on predefined criteria or adaptive learning. For example, the system may trigger alerts for **threshold violations** (e.g., temperature, pressure) or suggest corrective actions.
   - **Confidence Measures**: While not detailed, the DT’s decision-making confidence is likely derived from **data validation** (e.g., sensor accuracy, data consistency) and **model calibration** (e.g., alignment between physical and digital states). The standard emphasizes **functional fidelity** as a proxy for confidence.

3. **Decision-Making Processes**:
   - **Closed-Loop Control**: Decisions are executed in a **closed-loop system**, where the Digital Twin Entity’s recommendations (e.g., control commands) are sent to the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which actuates the OME accordingly. This ensures **real-time decision execution**.
   - **User-Driven Adjustments**: The **User Entity** provides interfaces for users to **override or refine** decisions based on domain expertise. This hybrid approach combines **automated insights** with **human judgment**.
   - **Scenario-Based Decisions**: Decisions are context-aware, adapting to **operational scenarios** (e.g., emergency shutdowns, production line changes). The standard does not specify decision-making algorithms but implies **adaptive logic** to handle dynamic conditions.

4. **User Interaction Methods**:
   - **Monitoring Interfaces**: Users interact with the DT via **dashboard-like interfaces** provided by the User Entity, which display real-time data, insights, and alerts. The standard does not detail specific UI/UX tools but suggests **human-centric design** for intuitive interaction.
   - **Control Interfaces**: Users can **manually intervene** in the DT’s decision-making process, adjusting parameters or overriding automated actions. This is facilitated by **FE-based interfaces** that bridge the gap between human input and system control.
   - **Feedback Loops**: User feedback is incorporated into the DT’s evolution, enabling **iterative improvement** of insights and decision-making processes. The standard does not specify feedback mechanisms but highlights the **collaborative nature** of user-DT interaction.

5. **Confidence Measures for Recommendations**:
   - **Data Integrity Validation**: Confidence in recommendations is tied to the **validity and consistency** of data collected by the Data Collection Entity. Anomalies or inconsistencies may trigger **alerts or recalibration** of the DT’s model.
   - **Model Alignment**: Confidence is also derived from the **degree of alignment** between the Digital Twin Entity’s model and the OME’s physical state. Discrepancies may indicate **model drift** or **sensor inaccuracies**, requiring corrective action.
   - **Not in Document**: Specific quantitative confidence metrics (e.g., confidence intervals, probability scores) are not mentioned in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin (DT) system described in **ISO 23247** adheres to a **comprehensive standardization framework**, primarily structured around **industry-specific standards, interoperability protocols, and modular entity-based architectures**. The standard defines compliance requirements, interoperability guidelines, and certification implications for manufacturing-focused DTs.

1. **Relevant Industry Standards**:
   - **ISO 23247**: The foundational standard for DTs in manufacturing, divided into four parts:
     - **Part 1**: Overview and general principles (not detailed in the provided context).
     - **Part 2**: **Reference Architecture (RA)**, which defines the **entity-based framework** (OME, Data Collection, Digital Twin, User Entities) and their interactions. This is the most relevant part for implementation.
     - **Part 3**: Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements (not detailed here).
     - **Part 4**: Information exchange standards (not detailed here).
   - **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)**: While not explicitly mandated by ISO 23247, the standard **suggests OPC UA** as a protocol for **interoperability between Functional Entities (FEs)** and other system components. OPC UA ensures **secure, platform-independent data exchange** and is widely adopted in industrial automation.

2. **Specifications Followed**:
   - **Entity-Based Modularity**: The DT system follows a **modular specification**, where each entity (OME, Data Collection, Digital Twin, User) is designed to **implement specific functionalities** without tight coupling. This aligns with **service-oriented architecture (SOA)** principles.
   - **Data Collection Standards**: The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** must adhere to **industrial data standards** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) for **real-time data acquisition** and **device control**. The standard does not specify exact protocols but emphasizes **standardized interfaces**.
   - **Digital Representation Standards**: The **Digital Twin Entity** must maintain a **consistent digital representation** of the OME, adhering to **semantic and syntactic standards** for interoperability. This includes **data modeling** (e.g., using **ISO 15926** for product lifecycle data, though not explicitly mentioned).

3. **Certification Requirements**:
   - **ISO 23247 Compliance**: While not explicitly stated, adherence to **ISO 23247** implies **certification pathways** for DT systems in manufacturing. Compliance ensures **interoperability, reliability, and traceability** of the DT’s lifecycle.
   - **Functional Validation**: The standard implies **certification of functional entities** (FEs) to verify their performance, interoperability, and alignment with the DT’s overall architecture. No specific certification bodies or processes are mentioned.
   - **Not in Document**: No details are provided on **third-party certification** (e.g., ISO 9001, IEC 62443) or **regulatory compliance** (e.g., GDPR for data handling).

4. **Compliance Frameworks**:
   - **Entity Governance**: Compliance is managed through **clear governance of each entity’s responsibilities**, ensuring that:
     - The **Data Collection Entity** adheres to **data integrity and security standards**.
     - The **Digital Twin Entity** maintains **model accuracy and consistency**.
     - The **User Entity** provides **secure and intuitive interfaces**.
   - **Interoperability Frameworks**: The standard emphasizes **interoperability between entities** via **standardized interfaces** (e.g., OPC UA). This ensures seamless data flow and **cross-system compatibility**.

5. **Interoperability Standards Adopted**:
   - **OPC UA**: The primary interoperability standard suggested for **data exchange and device control** between FEs and other entities. OPC UA supports **real-time synchronization**, **security**, and **platform independence**.
   - **Modular Interfaces**: The DT’s architecture relies on **well-defined interfaces** for each entity, enabling **plug-and-play compatibility** with other systems. This aligns with **industrial IoT (IIoT) interoperability standards**.
   - **Not in Document**: No mention of **cloud interoperability standards** (e.g., OASIS, DDS), **edge computing standards**, or **semantic interoperability frameworks** (e.g., ontologies like **OWL, RDF**).
}
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system described in the provided documents incorporates **ISO 26262-1:2018** functional safety standards for **road vehicles**, mandating rigorous cybersecurity and safety protocols. Key measures include:
- **Cybersecurity Protocols**: Adherence to **ISO 26262-8** (automotive-specific cybersecurity requirements) for secure communication and data integrity, ensuring protection against unauthorized access and tampering in **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) services** (as referenced in **3GPP TS 22.185 v15.0.0**).
- **Access Control Mechanisms**: Role-based access control (RBAC) and **authentication systems** (e.g., **EAP-AKA’** for V2X services) to enforce granular permissions for system components, as outlined in **3GPP TS 22.185**.
- **Threat Mitigation Strategies**: Implementation of **fail-safe mechanisms** (e.g., redundant system checks, graceful degradation) to comply with **ISO 26262-4** for safety-critical systems, ensuring continuous operation under fault conditions.
- **Risk Assessment Approaches**: Systematic **risk analysis** (e.g., **FMEA - Failure Mode and Effects Analysis**) per **ISO 26262-5** to identify vulnerabilities in the Digital Twin’s **interactive cockpit models** (as discussed in **Dalibor et al., 2020**), prioritizing mitigation for high-severity threats.
- **Safety-Critical System Considerations**: Integration of **safety mechanisms** (e.g., watchdog timers, hardware redundancy) to prevent system failures from propagating, aligned with **ISO 26262-6** for automotive-grade reliability. The **model-driven architecture** (MDA) proposed in **Dalibor et al., 2020** emphasizes **safety-by-design** principles for Digital Twin cockpits, ensuring compliance with **ISO 26262-8** for cyber-physical system security.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**
block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
